curator: auto (generate_day.py — reals verified verbatim vs source + cross-validated)

quotes.text: The morning began, as all decent mornings do, with the deep and abiding suspicion that the toaster was working against me. I have never trusted an appliance that gets hot and makes decisions on my behalf. You put the bread in and you get back a VERDICT, and I have never once been found innocent. This is not breakfast. It is a hostile goddamn interrogation with a crumb tray, and I will not be the first to break.
quotes.speaker: Hunter S. Thompson
quotes.context: A characteristically unhinged 1998 column on domestic life
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the paranoid grandiosity aimed at a harmless appliance is the Hunter S. Thompson register. The tell: the real Hunter would've dragged a federal conspiracy into it by the second sentence; this one never leaves the kitchen.

quotes.text: Over the past forty years I've been loaded on booze, coke, acid, Quaaludes, glue, cough mixture, heroin, Rohypnol, Klonopin, Vicodin, and too many other heavy-duty substances to list in this footnote. On more than a few occasions I was on all of these at the same time.
quotes.speaker: Ozzy Osbourne
quotes.context: From a footnote in his 2010 autobiography 'I Am Ozzy' (with Chris Ayres), cataloguing four decades of his own substance abuse.

quotes.text: People ask me the secret to a long life. I tell 'em: ignore every goddamn thing your doctor says, marry somebody who can cook, and never, EVER trust a man who doesn't sweat. A dry man is hiding something — money, a second family, or a personality. That ain't wisdom, sweetheart, that's sixty years of paying attention and zero years of shutting up about it.
quotes.speaker: Redd Foxx
quotes.context: On longevity, a raunchy 1970s nightclub set
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the blunt, cantankerous life-advice with a filthy-adjacent kicker is Redd Foxx to the bone. The tell: the real Redd would've named the part and the price; this one keeps it almost gentle.

quotes.text: Frank used to call me at four in the morning to rehearse. Four! I'd pick up, listen for a second, and say Frank, it's a saloon song, not the goddamn moon landing — sing it sleepy, nobody's grading you. Then I'd hang up, roll over, and go back to doing absolutely nothing, which, for the record, I have turned into an art form that got three women to marry me and exactly none to stay.
quotes.speaker: Dean Martin
quotes.context: On his friendship with Sinatra, a 1970s variety-show chat
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the laid-back 'I've perfected doing nothing' shtick and the Sinatra needle are the Dean Martin persona. The tell: the real Dean would've landed the whole thing in half the words and a raised eyebrow.

quotes.text: People keep asking me the secret and they want it to be oatmeal. It's not oatmeal. It's a cigar, a martini I'm technically not supposed to have, and a young woman who laughs at my material whether or not it's any good. I've done the same act for sixty years. The act didn't get better — the audience just got too polite to walk out on a man my age.
quotes.speaker: George Burns
quotes.context: On longevity and showbiz, a 1980s interview
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the cigar-and-a-martini, randy-old-showman bit is exactly George Burns's lane. The tell: the real George would've delivered it slower, with a puff between every clause, and made the young woman a specific punchline.

quotes.text: There are 400,000 words in the English language, and there are seven of them you can't say on television. What a ratio that is. 399,993 to 7. They must really be bad. Shit, piss, fuck, cunt, cocksucker, motherfucker, and tits.
quotes.speaker: George Carlin
quotes.context: Carlin's landmark "Seven Words You Can Never Say on Television" routine from his 1972 album Class Clown, listing the broadcast-banned words verbatim and reveling in saying them all out loud.

source.title: 'Class Clown' — Seven Words You Can Never Say on Television
